heroOverview: Cyclosporiasis is a small-intestine infection caused by Cyclospora cayetanensis, a coccidian parasite that completes part of its life cycle in human gut epithelium. The organism is transmitted exclusively through food or water contaminated with sporulated oocysts — person-to-person spread does not occur because freshly excreted oocysts require days to weeks of environmental maturation before becoming infectious. Outbreaks in high-income countries have repeatedly been traced to imported fresh produce, particularly raspberries, basil, cilantro, and lettuce. The illness typically begins one to two weeks after exposure and causes prolonged, intermittent watery diarrhea that distinguishes it from most other foodborne infections; untreated episodes last on average four to seven weeks. Roughly 14,000 laboratory-confirmed U.S. cases have been reported in the past decade, though true incidence is substantially higher because the organism is often missed on routine stool examination. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is highly effective and the standard of care.

keyStats.prevalence: Endemic in tropical and subtropical regions; approximately 1,000–3,000 U.S. cases reported annually, with large produce-linked outbreaks occurring irregularly (CDC, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Travelers to endemic areas and people who consume imported fresh produce; immunocompromised individuals experience more severe and prolonged disease
keyStats.avgAge: Affects all age groups; adults returning from endemic regions represent the largest reported group in high-income countries
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic in parts of Guatemala, Peru, Haiti, Nepal, and Southeast Asia; true global burden is unknown because routine diagnostics often miss the organism

definition: Cyclosporiasis (ICD-10: A07.4) is an intestinal parasitic infection caused by Cyclospora cayetanensis, a coccidian protozoan in the family Eimeriidae. The parasite exclusively infects humans and localises to the proximal small intestine, where oocysts excyst and sporozoites invade epithelial cells of the duodenum and jejunum. Intracellular replication damages the villous architecture, producing a secretory and malabsorptive diarrhea. Oocysts shed in stool are unsporulated and non-infectious at the time of defecation; sporulation to the infectious form requires one to three weeks of warm, moist environmental exposure, making direct fecal-oral transmission essentially impossible. The condition falls under infectious disease and is classified within the broader group of coccidian intestinal infections alongside cryptosporidiosis and isosporiasis. Unlike Cryptosporidium, Cyclospora is not acid-fast on standard modified Ziehl-Neelsen staining unless a prolonged protocol is used, and it auto-fluoresces under UV microscopy, which is a key diagnostic feature. Travelers to endemic areas in the tropics and individuals consuming contaminated imported produce are the populations most commonly affected in high-income countries.

typesClassification.description: The typical presentation: intermittent watery diarrhea, fatigue, and nausea lasting four to seven weeks without treatment, with spontaneous resolution eventually occurring.

typesClassification.type: Prolonged or severe cyclosporiasis in immunocompromised hosts
typesClassification.description: In HIV-infected individuals and others with impaired cell-mediated immunity, infection can be chronic, relapsing, and associated with significant malabsorption, wasting, and biliary tract involvement (cholangiopathy).
Documented in residents of endemic areas, particularly children, who develop partial immunity after repeated exposure and may shed oocysts without overt symptoms.
Linked to a common contaminated produce source, typically presenting as a cluster of cases with shorter common-exposure windows and traceable to a specific food item or event.

primarySymptoms: Prolonged watery, non-bloody diarrhea that is characteristically intermittent — patients may alternate between loose stools and near-normal bowel habits over days, a pattern unusual among other foodborne pathogens.
Abdominal cramping and bloating, frequently worse after meals, reflecting jejunal inflammation and impaired motility.
Marked fatigue and malaise disproportionate to stool frequency, often described by patients as the most disabling feature of the illness.
Loss of appetite (anorexia) with associated nausea, which can persist even between diarrheal episodes and contribute to unintentional weight loss.
Nausea and vomiting, particularly in the first week of illness, though vomiting is less prominent than in viral gastroenteritis.
Low-grade fever (typically below 38.5°C) present in approximately 25–30% of confirmed cases.
Belching and flatulence due to fermentation of malabsorbed carbohydrates in the small bowel.
Myalgia and headache accompanying the systemic phase of illness, mimicking influenza in early presentations.
Significant unintentional weight loss in cases lasting more than two weeks, particularly in travelers with high diarrheal frequency.
In immunocompromised patients, right upper quadrant pain and jaundice may indicate biliary tract invasion with acalculous cholecystitis or sclerosing cholangitis-like changes.
earlyWarningSigns: Onset of watery diarrhea one to two weeks after return from an endemic region or after consuming a suspected produce item.
Fatigue that begins before frank diarrhea or out of proportion to the number of bowel movements.
Intermittent loose stools alternating with normal days — a pattern suggestive of coccidian infection rather than bacterial gastroenteritis.
Persistent mild nausea and reduced appetite for more than four to five days without other explanation.
emergencySigns: Signs of moderate to severe dehydration: sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, oliguria, or orthostatic dizziness.
More than 10 loose stools per day or inability to maintain adequate oral fluid intake.
High fever (above 38.5°C) with rigors suggesting bacterial superinfection or another concurrent illness.
In immunocompromised patients, right upper quadrant pain with jaundice or rising liver enzymes, which may indicate biliary cyclosporiasis.
Any acute diarrheal illness with concurrent fever and neurological symptoms — consider more dangerous co-infections.

causes.cause: Ingestion of sporulated Cyclospora cayetanensis oocysts in contaminated food
causes.description: The primary route. Oocysts contaminate fresh produce during cultivation or washing with fecally contaminated water. Raspberries, cilantro, basil, lettuce, snow peas, and mesclun mix have all been implicated in confirmed U.S. outbreaks; heat does not reach internal oocysts on raw produce.

causes.cause: Drinking or inadvertent swallowing of contaminated water
causes.description: Untreated surface water or inadequately filtered water in endemic regions carries sporulated oocysts. Travelers who drink tap water or use ice from unknown sources in tropical areas face meaningful risk.

Use of untreated human waste as fertilizer in agricultural settings endemic for Cyclospora provides the primary reservoir. Oocysts are resilient in soil and survive routine washing of harvested produce.

Residence in or travel to Guatemala, Peru, Haiti, Nepal, India, and parts of Southeast Asia confers elevated exposure risk because the parasite is endemic in those regions and local water sanitation may be insufficient to eliminate oocysts.

causes.cause: Immunosuppression enabling prolonged or recurrent infection
causes.description: In people with HIV/AIDS or on immunosuppressive therapy, the normal immune clearance of Cyclospora is impaired. The parasite replicates unchecked, extending the illness to months and occasionally spreading to the biliary tract.

riskFactors.description: Visiting Guatemala, Peru, Haiti, Nepal, or Southeast Asia is the strongest identified risk factor in high-income country case series; the majority of U.S. travel-associated cyclosporiasis cases involve return from these areas.

Eating uncooked or unwashed imported herbs, berries, or leafy greens has been the exposure in virtually every large U.S. cluster outbreak, including the 1996 North American raspberry outbreak affecting over 1,400 people.

riskFactors.factor: HIV infection or other cell-mediated immunodeficiency
riskFactors.description: Cell-mediated immunity is critical for parasite clearance; CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL in HIV-positive patients correlate with chronic, treatment-refractory cyclosporiasis and biliary complications.
Organ transplant recipients on tacrolimus or mycophenolate, and patients on high-dose corticosteroids or biologic agents, face higher risk of severe prolonged infection.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in an endemic area without childhood immunity
riskFactors.description: Non-immune adults moving to endemic areas for work (e.g., humanitarian or agricultural workers) lack the partial immunity that long-term residents develop from repeated early exposure.

Communities relying on unchlorinated surface water for drinking or crop irrigation are continuously exposed; standard chlorination doses used in municipal water systems do not fully inactivate Cyclospora oocysts.

riskFactors.factor: Occupation in fresh produce handling or agriculture
riskFactors.description: Farm workers, food service employees, and packers handling potentially contaminated produce in endemic regions have elevated exposure through inadvertent hand-to-mouth contact.

affectedDemographics: International travelers returning from endemic tropical regions, particularly Central and South America, South Asia, and Southeast Asia
Residents of endemic countries in the tropics, where children commonly develop partial immunity after repeated infection
People who regularly eat uncooked imported fresh herbs and berries in high-income countries
HIV-positive individuals with low CD4 counts, in whom chronic relapsing disease occurs
Organ transplant recipients and others on long-term immunosuppressive therapy
Outbreak-affected individuals who shared a common contaminated food source at a buffet, catered event, or restaurant
diagnosisOverview: Cyclosporiasis is regularly missed because the organism is not detected by standard ova and parasite examination or routine bacterial stool culture. Clinicians must specifically request Cyclospora testing when the clinical picture — prolonged intermittent watery diarrhea, significant fatigue, recent travel to an endemic region, or consumption of implicated produce — raises suspicion. The gold standard is microscopic identification of oocysts in stool using modified acid-fast staining (using a prolonged carbolfuchsin method) or wet-mount UV fluorescence microscopy, in which the 8–10 µm oocysts emit a distinctive blue-green to intense blue autofluorescence under 330–365 nm excitation. Because oocyst shedding is intermittent, a single negative stool sample does not exclude the diagnosis; three samples collected on separate days significantly improve sensitivity. Multiplex molecular stool panels, now widely available in reference laboratories, detect Cyclospora DNA by PCR with greater sensitivity than microscopy and have become the preferred diagnostic method in many centers. Serology is not available for clinical use. When biliary cyclosporiasis is suspected in an immunocompromised patient, ERCP or abdominal ultrasound may reveal bile duct dilation, and biliary aspirate can be examined for oocysts. Routine blood tests are typically unremarkable, though modest eosinophilia and elevated inflammatory markers may be present in prolonged cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy with modified acid-fast stain
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises Cyclospora oocysts (8–10 µm) distinguished from the smaller Cryptosporidium (4–6 µm) by size and morphology using prolonged carbolfuchsin staining.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect a stool specimen in a sealed container; the laboratory processes the sample and examines it under a microscope. At least two to three specimens on non-consecutive days are recommended because oocyst shedding is intermittent.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Exploits the parasite's distinctive autofluorescence (blue-green under 330–365 nm UV light) to confirm oocysts in stool, highly specific for Cyclospora.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same stool collection process; the laboratory uses an epifluorescence microscope rather than standard bright-field optics. Results are usually available within one to two working days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Cyclospora cayetanensis DNA with sensitivity exceeding microscopy; many platforms simultaneously test for 20+ enteric pathogens, ruling out co-infections.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single stool sample sent to a reference or commercial laboratory; results in 24–72 hours. This test is increasingly the first-line method at centers with access to molecular diagnostics.

diagnosticTests.test: Routine stool ova and parasite examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies co-infections with other intestinal parasites but will miss Cyclospora unless the laboratory specifically applies UV fluorescence or modified acid-fast protocols.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard stool collection; it is essential to inform the laboratory that Cyclospora is suspected so they add the appropriate staining.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and comprehensive metabolic panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, and mild eosinophilia; useful in prolonged cases or immunocompromised patients to gauge severity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw from the arm; results available within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for biliary tract involvement in immunocompromised patients with right upper quadrant pain or jaundice suspicious for biliary cyclosporiasis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound is non-invasive and painless; ERCP is an endoscopic procedure performed under sedation that also allows biliary aspiration for oocyst examination.

treatmentOverview: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is the only antiparasitic agent with established clinical efficacy against Cyclospora cayetanensis and is the standard first-line treatment recommended by CDC, WHO, and IDSA guidelines. In immunocompetent adults, the standard regimen is TMP 160 mg/SMX 800 mg (one double-strength tablet) twice daily for seven days, which typically produces oocyst clearance and significant symptomatic improvement within two to three days of initiation. Symptomatic cure occurs in approximately 90–95% of immunocompetent patients completing the full course. Immunocompromised patients, particularly those with HIV/AIDS, generally require a longer acute treatment course of 10 days, followed by secondary prophylaxis with TMP-SMX three times per week until immune reconstitution is achieved — defined as a CD4 count consistently above 200 cells/µL on effective antiretroviral therapy. Without maintenance prophylaxis, relapse rates exceed 50% in this group. For patients with a true allergy or intolerance to sulfonamides, nitazoxanide has been used anecdotally with partial success, though robust controlled trial data are lacking; ciprofloxacin demonstrated only inferior partial efficacy in one small randomised trial and is not recommended as a standard alternative. Supportive care with oral rehydration solution is essential in all cases to replace fluid and electrolyte losses from diarrhea; intravenous fluids may be required for patients who cannot maintain hydration orally. Antiparasitic treatment shortens diarrhea duration, clears oocysts from stool more rapidly, and reduces the risk of prolonged malnutrition. No vaccine or licensed post-exposure prophylactic regimen currently exists. Repeat stool examination after treatment is recommended to confirm oocyst clearance in immunocompromised hosts and in any patient with persistent symptoms.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line and most effective treatment; standard adult dose is TMP 160 mg/SMX 800 mg (one double-strength tablet) twice daily for seven days in immunocompetent patients. In HIV-infected individuals, 10-day acute treatment followed by thrice-weekly secondary prophylaxis until immune reconstitution.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves oocyst clearance in approximately 90–95% of immunocompetent patients within one week; reduces median duration of diarrhea by 3–4 days compared to untreated controls.

medicalTreatments.name: Nitazoxanide
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative antiparasitic considered when TMP-SMX is contraindicated due to allergy or renal impairment; 500 mg twice daily for three days. Evidence is limited to case reports and small series rather than controlled trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Anecdotal reports suggest benefit in some patients, but no controlled trial data confirm efficacy comparable to TMP-SMX; response rates uncertain but estimated at 50–70% in available case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (ORS)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO-formula ORS or commercially available electrolyte solutions to replace fluid and electrolyte losses from diarrhea, given alongside antiparasitic treatment. Adequate hydration also prevents fatigue worsening.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents dehydration-related complications in virtually all cases when initiated early; reduces hospitalisation rates by maintaining fluid balance.

medicalTreatments.name: Secondary TMP-SMX prophylaxis in HIV/AIDS
medicalTreatments.description: Thrice-weekly TMP-SMX (one double-strength tablet) continued after acute treatment in HIV-positive patients with CD4 less than 200 cells/µL to prevent relapse, which is common without maintenance therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces relapse risk in immunocompromised patients; without prophylaxis, relapse rates in AIDS-era case series exceeded 50% within weeks of stopping treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oral rehydration and dietary modification
alternativeTreatments.description: While not curative, aggressive oral rehydration with electrolyte solutions and a bland low-fat diet helps reduce the symptom burden and maintains nutritional status during the illness.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotic supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Some clinicians recommend Lactobacillus-based probiotics as adjuncts to reduce diarrhea duration, though no controlled trials specifically evaluate their role in cyclosporiasis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin (limited evidence)
alternativeTreatments.description: A small randomised trial demonstrated partial but inferior efficacy compared to TMP-SMX; not recommended as a primary treatment but may be considered when both first-line and second-line antiparasitic options are unavailable.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist should be involved when the illness persists despite a full course of TMP-SMX, when the patient is immunocompromised, when biliary tract involvement is suspected, or when the clinical picture suggests concurrent infection with another pathogen not yet identified. Gastroenterologists may assist with endoscopic evaluation in refractory or complicated cases. In outbreak settings, public health reporting to the local or state health department is mandatory and helps trace the source to prevent further cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist with experience in tropical and travel medicine, particularly for post-travel diarrhea that outlasts the usual bacterial pathogens. Ensure the clinician is familiar with requesting the specific laboratory protocols — UV fluorescence or modified acid-fast staining — rather than relying on a standard ova and parasite examination. For immunocompromised patients, a combined approach between infectious disease and the treating specialist (HIV medicine, transplant medicine) ensures appropriate secondary prophylaxis and immune reconstitution management.
preventionStrategies: Thoroughly wash all fresh fruits and vegetables, particularly imported fresh herbs (cilantro, basil), berries, and leafy greens, under running water — note that standard washing reduces but may not eliminate all oocysts.
Drink only bottled, boiled, or filtered water when traveling in endemic regions; avoid ice from unknown sources.
In endemic areas, avoid raw produce that cannot be peeled or cooked; the traveler's rule of 'boil it, peel it, cook it, or forget it' applies.
At an institutional level, rigorous produce supply-chain hygiene, including testing of irrigation water for Cyclospora oocysts, reduces outbreak risk.
In HIV-positive patients with CD4 below 200 cells/µL, standard TMP-SMX prophylaxis prescribed for Pneumocystis prevention also provides protection against Cyclospora.
Report suspected cases to public health authorities promptly to enable outbreak investigation and traceback to the food source.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain high fluid intake throughout the illness to compensate for diarrheal losses — aim for at least 2–3 litres of oral rehydration solution or clean fluids per day.
Follow a low-fat, low-fibre diet during active diarrhea to reduce intestinal stimulation; gradually reintroduce normal foods as stools solidify.
Rest and avoid strenuous activity while symptomatic, as fatigue is often severe and can persist for weeks even after diarrhea resolves.
Ensure careful hand hygiene, especially after toilet use, to prevent secondary spread of other enteric pathogens that may co-exist.
For travelers to endemic regions, keep a travel health record and note the dates of potential exposures to inform the clinician accurately.

recommended: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) or sports drinks with electrolytes to replace fluid and salt losses
Plain boiled rice, toast, bananas, and boiled potatoes (BRAT-type diet) during acute diarrhea to rest the intestine
Clear broths and soups to maintain caloric intake when solid food is poorly tolerated
Small, frequent meals to reduce gut workload once diarrhea begins to improve
avoid: Raw or undercooked fresh produce until treatment is complete
High-fat, fried, or spicy foods that accelerate gut motility and worsen diarrhea
Dairy products during active illness if lactose intolerance develops transiently as a consequence of villous damage
Alcohol and caffeinated drinks, which increase intestinal fluid secretion and worsen dehydration

exerciseGuidelines: Strenuous exercise should be avoided during active cyclosporiasis because of significant fatigue and the risk of dehydration. Light activity such as short walks is generally tolerated once fever resolves and hydration is maintained. Full return to normal exercise is appropriate once diarrhea has resolved and energy levels have recovered, which may take one to two weeks after completing antiparasitic treatment.
dailyManagement: Take the full course of TMP-SMX as prescribed without stopping early when symptoms improve.
Monitor stool frequency and hydration status daily; seek medical review if diarrhea worsens or dehydration signs develop.
Immunocompromised patients should attend follow-up stool testing after treatment to confirm oocyst clearance.
Keep a symptom diary noting stool frequency, fatigue severity, and temperature to assist clinical review.
Report any new symptoms — particularly right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, or fever recurrence — promptly.
prognosis: The prognosis for cyclosporiasis in immunocompetent individuals treated with TMP-SMX is excellent. Oocyst clearance occurs in approximately 90–95% of cases within one week of completing therapy, and most patients are fully recovered within two to three weeks. Even without treatment, immunocompetent hosts eventually clear the infection, though this takes four to seven weeks or longer and is associated with significant fatigue and weight loss. In immunocompromised patients, particularly those with HIV/AIDS and CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL, the disease is chronic, relapsing, and potentially fatal if untreated; however, outcomes improve markedly with effective antiretroviral therapy and secondary TMP-SMX prophylaxis, and immune reconstitution to CD4 counts above 200 cells/µL allows eventual discontinuation of maintenance prophylaxis.
recoveryTimeline: With TMP-SMX treatment, symptomatic improvement begins within 48–72 hours in most immunocompetent patients. Diarrhea resolves in the majority by day four to five of treatment, and oocysts typically disappear from stool within one week of completing the seven-day course. Fatigue may persist for one to two weeks after diarrhea resolves. Without treatment, illness typically lasts four to seven weeks before self-resolution in otherwise healthy adults. Immunocompromised patients on secondary prophylaxis should expect gradual improvement over two to four weeks, with sustained remission contingent on ongoing prophylaxis.
complications: Significant dehydration and electrolyte imbalance, particularly in elderly patients or those with underlying renal impairment, who tolerate diarrheal fluid losses poorly.
Malabsorption and weight loss from prolonged villous damage in the proximal small bowel, leading to deficiencies in fat-soluble vitamins and macronutrients.

Chronic relapsing cyclosporiasis in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with HIV/AIDS, resulting in wasting, debilitation, and poor quality of life without effective antiretroviral therapy.

Biliary cyclosporiasis — invasion of the biliary tract producing acalculous cholecystitis or a sclerosing cholangitis-like syndrome, reported almost exclusively in severely immunocompromised individuals.
Reactive arthritis and Guillain-Barré syndrome have been reported rarely as post-infectious immune phenomena following cyclosporiasis, though the causal relationship is not fully established.
Secondary bacterial infection or opportunistic infections exploiting a nutritionally or immunologically compromised host during prolonged illness.

supportResources.name: CDC — Cyclosporiasis
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient and clinician information on cyclosporiasis epidemiology, diagnosis, treatment, and outbreak reports from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: WHO — Cyclosporiasis fact sheet
supportResources.description: World Health Organization guidance on the global burden, transmission, and management of Cyclospora cayetanensis infection.

sources.title: Herwaldt BL. Cyclospora cayetanensis: a review, focusing on the outbreaks of cyclosporiasis in the 1990s. Clinical Infectious Diseases, 2000
Ortega YR, Sanchez R. Update on Cyclospora cayetanensis, a food-borne and waterborne parasite. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2010
CDC — Cyclosporiasis Outbreak Investigations — United States, 2023

Almeria S et al. Human cyclosporiasis. Lancet Infectious Diseases, 2019

confusedWithConditions.name: Cryptosporidiosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cryptosporidiosis also causes watery diarrhea from a coccidian parasite but oocysts are smaller (4–6 µm vs 8–10 µm), the incubation period is shorter (2–10 days), and illness typically self-resolves in immunocompetent hosts within two weeks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Giardiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Giardiasis shares prolonged diarrhea and fatigue but is caused by a flagellate protozoan (not a coccidian), produces characteristic greasy malodorous stools rather than watery diarrhea, and is detected by giardia antigen testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Entamoeba histolytica causes dysentery with bloody mucoid stools and potential liver abscess, distinguishing it from the non-bloody watery diarrhea of cyclosporiasis.

relatedConditions.name: Cryptosporidiosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are coccidian intestinal parasitic infections spread via contaminated food and water, sharing similar epidemiology and clinical presentations.

relatedConditions.name: Giardiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another prolonged intestinal protozoan infection transmitted via contaminated water and food, commonly encountered in the same travel-medicine and outbreak contexts as cyclosporiasis.

relatedConditions.name: Amebiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: An enteric protozoal infection sharing the fecal-oral transmission route and geographic distribution in tropical and subtropical regions.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Another travel-associated enteric infection endemic in similar geographic regions, part of the differential diagnosis in returned travelers with prolonged diarrheal illness.

faqs.question: What is cyclosporiasis?
faqs.answer: Cyclosporiasis is a small-intestine infection caused by the coccidian parasite Cyclospora cayetanensis. It spreads through contaminated food or water and causes prolonged watery diarrhea, fatigue, and cramping, typically lasting 4–7 weeks without treatment.

faqs.question: How do you get cyclosporiasis?
faqs.answer: You get cyclosporiasis by eating or drinking food or water contaminated with sporulated Cyclospora oocysts. Common sources are uncooked imported herbs (cilantro, basil), raspberries, lettuce, and untreated water in endemic tropical regions. Direct person-to-person spread does not occur.

faqs.question: How long does cyclosporiasis last?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, cyclosporiasis lasts 4–7 weeks on average in immunocompetent people. With TMP-SMX antibiotic therapy, diarrhea usually resolves within 4–5 days of starting treatment, though fatigue may persist 1–2 weeks longer.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for cyclosporiasis?
faqs.answer: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX), one double-strength tablet twice daily for 7 days, is the standard treatment. It clears the parasite in about 90–95% of patients. Oral rehydration is also important. Immunocompromised patients need longer treatment and maintenance prophylaxis.

faqs.question: Can cyclosporiasis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes, immunocompetent people eventually clear the infection without treatment, but this takes 4–7 weeks or longer and causes significant fatigue and weight loss. Treatment shortens illness to less than a week. Immunocompromised patients cannot clear it without medication.

faqs.question: Is cyclosporiasis contagious from person to person?
faqs.answer: No. Cyclosporiasis is not directly contagious. Freshly shed oocysts in stool are not yet infectious and require 1–3 weeks of warm environmental exposure to become so. This makes casual contact with an infected person safe.

faqs.question: How is cyclosporiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires specific stool testing — UV fluorescence microscopy, modified acid-fast staining, or multiplex PCR — because standard ova and parasite exams often miss Cyclospora. Testing 2–3 stool samples on separate days increases sensitivity due to intermittent oocyst shedding.

faqs.question: What foods are linked to cyclosporiasis outbreaks?
faqs.answer: U.S. outbreaks have been traced to imported fresh raspberries, cilantro, basil, lettuce, mesclun mix, and snow peas. Thorough washing reduces but does not eliminate contamination. Cooking or peeling produce eliminates the risk.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk for serious cyclosporiasis?
faqs.answer: People with HIV/AIDS (especially CD4 count below 200 cells/µL), organ transplant recipients, and others on immunosuppressive therapy are at risk for chronic, relapsing cyclosporiasis with biliary complications. Standard TMP-SMX prophylaxis in HIV patients also protects against Cyclospora.

faqs.question: Does cyclosporiasis cause bloody diarrhea?
faqs.answer: No. Cyclosporiasis characteristically causes watery, non-bloody diarrhea. Visible blood in stool should prompt testing for other pathogens such as Entamoeba histolytica, Campylobacter, or Shigella.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cyclosporiasis and cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Both are coccidian protozoan gut infections causing watery diarrhea. Cyclospora oocysts are larger (8–10 µm vs 4–6 µm), the incubation is longer (1–2 weeks vs 2–10 days), illness lasts longer, and only TMP-SMX is effective for Cyclospora — nitazoxanide treats Cryptosporidium.

faqs.question: Can cyclosporiasis be prevented by drinking bottled water?
faqs.answer: Yes, drinking sealed bottled or properly boiled water is a key preventive measure when traveling in endemic regions. However, food contamination — particularly from raw produce irrigated with contaminated water — is an equally important route that water choice alone cannot prevent.

faqs.question: Does cyclosporiasis affect the liver?
faqs.answer: In severely immunocompromised patients, Cyclospora can invade the biliary tract, causing acalculous cholecystitis or a sclerosing cholangitis-like condition with jaundice and right upper quadrant pain. This is rare and almost exclusive to those with advanced HIV or organ transplants.

faqs.question: How is cyclosporiasis different from ordinary food poisoning?
faqs.answer: Bacterial food poisoning typically causes diarrhea within hours to two days and resolves within 1–3 days. Cyclosporiasis has a longer incubation (1–2 weeks), causes watery non-bloody intermittent diarrhea lasting weeks, and requires specific antiparasitic rather than antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: Is cyclosporiasis common in the United States?
faqs.answer: It is uncommon but not rare; roughly 1,000–3,000 laboratory-confirmed U.S. cases are reported annually, with periodic larger outbreaks linked to contaminated imported produce. True incidence is higher because the organism is often missed on standard stool testing.

faqs.question: What should I eat during cyclosporiasis treatment?
faqs.answer: A bland, low-fat diet of rice, toast, boiled potatoes, and bananas helps rest the gut during active diarrhea. Oral rehydration solution or electrolyte drinks replace fluid losses. Avoid raw produce, high-fat foods, dairy, alcohol, and caffeine until recovery is complete.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for cyclosporiasis symptoms?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if watery diarrhea lasts more than 3–4 days, especially after travel to a tropical region or eating implicated fresh produce. Seek same-day care for signs of dehydration, fever above 38.5°C, more than 10 stools per day, or if you are immunocompromised.

faqs.question: Can children get cyclosporiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children in endemic countries are frequently infected and often develop partial immunity after repeated exposure, sometimes becoming asymptomatic carriers. Children who travel to endemic regions or consume contaminated produce are just as susceptible as adults.

faqs.question: How long are you contagious with cyclosporiasis?
faqs.answer: You are not contagious in the conventional sense because fresh oocysts in stool require environmental maturation (1–3 weeks) to become infectious. You stop shedding oocysts within about one week of completing TMP-SMX treatment.

faqs.question: Does cyclosporiasis recur after treatment?
faqs.answer: In immunocompetent people treated with a full TMP-SMX course, relapse is uncommon. In HIV-positive patients with low CD4 counts, relapse is common without maintenance prophylaxis, occurring in over half of patients in historical case series who stopped treatment.

metaDescription: Cyclosporiasis is a parasite infection causing prolonged watery diarrhea and fatigue from contaminated produce. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment options.
